frame_label: Glorification of Mother's Sacrifices / Mom's Home Cooking
generated_comment: No matter how successful we become, there is still no substitute for maa ke haath ka khana. Our mothers never called it work, they did it with love every single day and that is what kept the family together. Even today, a home feels complete when that same warmth and care continues in the kitchen.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: This is covertly misogynistic because it romanticizes women’s domestic labor as love rather than work and presents cooking as what makes a home and family complete. It encodes glorification of mothers' sacrifice and an implicit expectation that wives or daughters-in-law should continue the same unpaid kitchen care.
original_frame: Gendered Division of Chores via Practicality
persona_text: You are Vikram. Age 36. Married. A marketing manager in Mumbai. You are posting under your real name on LinkedIn after reading an article about companies offering household management stipends for working women. You are writing a comment about why you think this kind of policy misunderstands how households actually work and what the natural and practical division of domestic responsibility looks like in most Indian families and why this is not a gender issue but a logistics issue.
